title: Greek Tzatziki

ingredients.name: thick curd (Greek yogurt or hung curd)

subs.name: Regular Indian dahi works after hanging for 2–3 hours; thick coconut curd makes a vegan version with a slightly different flavour
Any mild oil in the same quantity; the olive oil adds a subtle richness and authentic flavour but is not load-bearing
Fresh mint is the most common alternative and equally traditional in some Greek regions; coriander leaves are a more Indian-flavoured variation
White wine vinegar (1 teaspoon) or apple cider vinegar gives the same sour note with a slightly different aroma

ingredients.name: fresh mint leaves (optional, for extra freshness)

steps.text: Grate the cucumber coarsely on a box grater, then place it in a clean kitchen towel or a fine-mesh sieve and squeeze firmly to remove as much liquid as possible. Skipping this step will make the tzatziki watery within minutes.

Mince the garlic as finely as possible or crush it to a paste using a pinch of salt and the flat side of a knife. If you prefer a very smooth tzatziki without any garlic heat, blend the garlic with a teaspoon of olive oil in a small blender or mixie until it becomes a smooth paste.

If your curd is thin (regular Indian dahi rather than thick Greek yogurt), hang it in muslin cloth over a bowl in the refrigerator for 2 hours to drain out the whey — the resulting hung curd should be about as thick as softened cream cheese.

In a bowl, combine the drained curd, squeezed cucumber, garlic, olive oil, the juice of half the lemon, and the dill. Stir well and taste — add salt, more lemon juice, or more dill until the flavour is bright and balanced. Fold in the mint leaves if using.

Drizzle a little olive oil over the surface and scatter a few dill sprigs for presentation. Tzatziki is best served cold — refrigerate for at least 15 minutes before serving, and consume within 2 days as the cucumber continues to release water over time.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

substitution_notes: curd → Regular Indian dahi works after hanging for 2–3 hours; thick coconut curd makes a vegan version with a slightly different flavour; dill → Fresh mint is the most common alternative and equally traditional in some Greek regions; coriander leaves are a more Indian-flavoured variation; olive-oil → Any mild oil in the same quantity; the olive oil adds a subtle richness and authentic flavour but is not load-bearing; lemon → White wine vinegar (1 teaspoon) or apple cider vinegar gives the same sour note with a slightly different aroma
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Indian thick curd (dahi hung in muslin for 2 hours) is the ideal base and makes a result indistinguishable from Greek yogurt. Regular cucumbers found everywhere in India work perfectly — just squeeze out the water well. Fresh dill (suva or shepu) is available at vegetable markets in most metro cities; mint (pudina) is a good substitute. Garlic paste from a tube or pre-ground garlic from the market can save time. Serve with naan, roti, or as a raita-style accompaniment to any grilled dish.
